content: {"answer":"The search results only partially address Dr. Tanaka's highly specific question. For HOMA-IR and fasting glucose in 16:8 time-restricted eating (TRE), Source 1 reports an overall improvement in fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C in participants following 16/8 TRE. Source 9 quantifies this, noting a significant reduction in fasting blood glucose of approximately -3.69 units versus usual diets. However, Source 5 counters that fasting glucose did not change across 2-12 month TRE trials in adults with obesity, and Source 6 (NEJM) found 16:8 TRE produced no greater weight or metabolic benefit than daily calorie restriction alone. Source 2 shows 5 weeks of early TRF (but not mid-day TRF) improved insulin sensitivity and reduced fasting plasma glucose, while Source 3 found 6-h early TRE did not improve fasting glucose but markedly decreased fasting insulin and insulin resistance - suggesting timing, not just duration, drives HOMA-IR effects.\n\nRegarding AMPK activation, mTORC1 suppression, and beta-hydroxybutyrate thresholds, the results do NOT provide the dose-response correlations requested. Source 10 shows fasting mice lacking skeletal muscle AMPK develop hyperketosis, implying AMPK-ketone coupling, and Source 12 describes an mTOR-autophagy axis regulated by intermittent fasting in skeletal muscle. Source 14 identifies LKB1 as the upstream kinase for alpha2-AMPK phosphorylation during fasting. Source 15 explicitly notes fasting-AMPK findings are inconsistent across human studies. Critically, NO source in this set reports beta-hydroxybutyrate thresholds in 16:8 RCTs, nor quantitative correlations between HOMA-IR improvements and AMPK/mTORC1 phosphorylation markers in humans. This mechanistic-clinical bridge is missing from the retrieved evidence and would require targeted searches of muscle biopsy TRE trials.","key_findings":["16:8 TRE improves HOMA-IR, fasting insulin, and fasting glucose per Source 1, with Source 9 reporting ~-3.69 unit fasting glucose reduction","Timing matters: early TRF improves insulin sensitivity (Source 2, 3) while mid-day TRF does not; Source 5 and Source 6 show null fasting-glucose effects in longer obesity trials","AMPK-MKO mice exhibit fasting-induced hyperketosis (Source 10), indicating AMPK gates ketone homeostasis, with LKB1 as upstream kinase (Source 14)","Intermittent fasting regulates the mTOR-autophagy axis in skeletal muscle (Source 12); sustained mTORC1 activation impairs autophagy (Source 13)","No retrieved source reports human 16:8 dose-response data for beta-hydroxybutyrate thresholds or quantitative AMPK/mTORC1 phosphorylation correlations with HOMA-IR"],"sources_used":["https://academic.oup.com/nutritionreviews/advance-article/doi/10.1093/nutrit/nuaf206/8373435","https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-28662-5","https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9400087/","https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004540","https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2114833","https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12829361/","https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5233441/","https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12486585/","https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413113001198","https://faseb.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1096/fj.201700442R","https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Effect-of-fasting-on-AMPK-signaling-in-skeletal-muscle-from-lean-and-obese-subjects-The_fig2_236064973"]}
